steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1's claim: *The Known World* is too rich to be called "historical fiction" because that label implies shallow "mere re-creation of the past". How would Text 2's author respond to this?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — By arguing that Jones displays a thorough knowledge of historical details in The Known World.: Text 2 explicitly says critics wrongly reduce historical fiction to "writers show off their knowledge of period details". Praising Jones for that would align with the shallow view Text 2 rejects. ✗
  • Choice B — By claiming that the author of Text 1 has underestimated the richness and complexity of The Known World.: Text 1 praises *The Known World* as "much richer and more complicated than the mere re-creation of the past". Text 2's author would have no reason to say Text 1 underestimates it. ✗
  • Choice C — By conceding that the genre of historical fiction contains many works that are less sophisticated than The Known World is.: Text 2 defends historical fiction broadly — it does not concede that the genre is often shallow. ✗
  • Choice D — By asserting that dissatisfaction with describing The Known World as historical fiction reflects a misunderstanding of the genre.: Text 2 argues critics misunderstand historical fiction — it can explore profound themes and complex characters. Text 1's discomfort with the label stems from seeing historical fiction as inherently shallow, exactly the misunderstanding Text 2 corrects. ✓
That is the correct answer.
